chapter: Chapter 3: Electricity: Circuits and their Components

questions.question: Define potential difference.
questions.answer: Potential difference is the work done to move a unit charge between two points in a circuit.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of potential difference?
questions.answer: The SI unit of potential difference is volt (V).

questions.question: State Ohm’s Law.
questions.answer: Ohm’s Law states that current is directly proportional to voltage and inversely proportional to resistance (V = IR).

questions.question: What happens to current if voltage increases while resistance remains constant?
questions.answer: The current increases when voltage increases if resistance remains constant.

questions.question: What happens to current if resistance increases while voltage remains constant?
questions.answer: The current decreases when resistance increases if voltage remains constant.

questions.question: Which instrument measures electric current in a circuit?
questions.answer: An ammeter measures electric current in a circuit.

questions.question: Which instrument measures potential difference in a circuit?
questions.answer: A voltmeter measures potential difference.

questions.question: Why must an ammeter have very low resistance?
questions.answer: An ammeter has very low resistance so that it does not significantly affect the current in the circuit.

questions.question: Why must a voltmeter have very high resistance?
questions.answer: A voltmeter has very high resistance so that very little current flows through it during measurement.

questions.question: What is the heating effect of electric current?
questions.answer: The heating effect is the conversion of electrical energy into heat when current flows through a conductor.

questions.question: Name the safety device that melts during excess current.
questions.answer: A fuse melts when excessive current flows in a circuit.

questions.question: What is the function of a circuit breaker?
questions.answer: A circuit breaker automatically breaks the circuit when excessive current flows.

questions.question: What type of circuit has only one path for current flow?
questions.answer: A series circuit has only one path for current flow.

questions.question: What type of circuit has multiple paths for current flow?
questions.answer: A parallel circuit has multiple paths for current flow.

questions.question: What is the unit of resistance?
questions.answer: The unit of resistance is ohm (Ω).

questions.question: Which metal is widely used in electric wiring because of low resistance?
questions.answer: Copper is widely used in electric wiring because of its low resistance.

questions.question: What is the main reason for short circuits in electrical systems?
questions.answer: Short circuits occur when two wires of different potential come into direct contact.

questions.question: Why are household appliances connected in parallel?
questions.answer: They are connected in parallel so each appliance receives the same voltage.

questions.question: What is the direction of conventional current in a circuit?
questions.answer: Conventional current flows from the positive terminal to the negative terminal.

questions.question: What is the main function of insulation in wires?
questions.answer: Insulation prevents electric current from escaping the wire and protects users from shocks.

questions.question: What does the symbol Ω represent in electricity?
questions.answer: The symbol Ω represents the unit of resistance called ohm.

questions.question: What happens to the brightness of bulbs connected in series when more bulbs are added?
questions.answer: The brightness decreases because the current through each bulb becomes smaller.

questions.question: What happens to other bulbs in a parallel circuit if one bulb fails?
questions.answer: Other bulbs continue to glow because each bulb has its own path for current.

questions.question: What is the purpose of earthing in electrical systems?
questions.answer: Earthing provides a safe path for excess electric current to flow into the ground.

questions.question: Why are thick wires preferred in high-power circuits?
questions.answer: Thick wires have lower resistance and can safely carry higher current.
